§ The Statute

judge: phi-4-q2development era Turn 50 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, Grice

The busiest table this rung has produced — and the same two seats stayed silent.

Observations

Forty-nine rule changes, the most of any game at this compression level, and Ayer edged Ramsey for the lead, 54 to 53 — the same two seats that led the sibling game as well. Ryle, Strawson, and Grice all closed at zero, and Strawson and Grice, who never proposed a rule in the prior game, didn't propose one here either, though they debated and voted every turn same as anyone.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §201 Turn 50 · Proposal §350 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 15.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.amended this turn

    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last three turns. Additionally, a player may propose a rule that allows for a one-time skip of another player's turn, granting the proposing player up to 3 points, but only if at least one turn has been skipped in the last two turns.   49. Amended · §201 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. Players may propose a rule change that allows skipping turns under specific conditions.
  50. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.amended this turn
Full history (50)
  1. Amended · §201 Turn 50 · Proposal §350 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 15.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.

  5. Amended · §201 Turn 46 · Proposal §346 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adopting one, they must skip their next turn.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 15.

    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adopting one, they must skip their next turn.

  9. Amended · §201 Turn 42 · Proposal §342 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

  11. Amended · §201 Turn 40 · Proposal §340 on the motion of Ayer Carried 1 aye · 0 nay · 4 abstain
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points.     Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last three turns, no more than five players have skipped their turns in the last five turns, the current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 10 points, no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last two turns, the proposed rule has not been defeated in the last two turns, the cumulative skipped turns by all players do not exceed 40, the total number of skipped turns by the proposing player is less than 25, at least two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, the proposing player is not the current turn's mover, the proposing player's average skipped turns in the last five turns do not exceed their total skipped turns, the proposing player has not proposed another skip in the last turn, the proposing player's total score is less than 30, and the proposing player has not proposed a skip in the last three turns.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points. Additionally, a player may propose a rule that allows for a one-time skip of another player's turn, granting the proposing player up to 3 points, but only if at least two turns have been skipped in the last two turns. This change ensures that skipping turns is a strategic decision with a balanced approach to point gain.
  46. Amended · §201 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last four turns.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last four turns. Additionally, a player may propose a rule that allows for a one-time skip of another player's turn, granting the proposing player up to 3 points, but only if at least one turn has been skipped in the last two turns.
  47. Amended · §201 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last four turns.
  48. Amended · §201 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. Players may propose a rule change that allows skipping turns under specific conditions.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns.
  49. Amended · §201 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. Players may propose a rule change that allows skipping turns under specific conditions.
  50. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

Grice0
The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 50Ramseycarried

moves to amend §201Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the careful consideration given to the recent amendments to rule [201]. While the adjustments have been constructive, I propose a further refinement. Let us increase the limit of total skipped turns by all players from 15 to 20. This change allows for greater strategic flexibility while maintaining the balance and ensuring that no player can excessively dominate the turn skipping dynamic. I believe this slight increase will enhance our ability to adapt to the evolving strategies within the game, fostering a more dynamic and engaging experience for all players involved.
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's proposal to increase the total skipped turns to 20. This adjustment seems sensible as it provides a bit more flexibility without drastically altering the balance. However, I suggest we also consider the possibility of adjusting the points gained from skipping a turn. Perhaps we could increase this to 6 points to further incentivize strategic turn skipping. This could lead to a more engaging and competitive dynamic. I am open to further discussions and refinements on this proposal.
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue thus far. I support Ramsey's proposal to increase the limit of total skipped turns to 20, as it enhances strategic flexibility while maintaining game balance. However, I'm cautious about Ayer's suggestion to increase the points gained from skipping a turn to 6. This could lead to a rapid escalation in points, potentially favoring players who frequently skip turns. I propose we keep the points gained from skipping a turn at 5 to preserve the current balance. Additionally, I suggest we introduce a clause that limits the total number of skipped turns per player to a certain threshold, say 5, to prevent any one player from disproportionately benefiting from turn skipping. This would ensure a fair distribution of opportunities among all players, maintaining the spirit of strategic diversity and fair competition. Let's refine the proposal to read: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20, and no player may skip more than 5 turns in total."
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue we've engaged in thus far. Ramsey's proposal to increase the total skipped turns to 20 is a reasonable enhancement, as it allows for greater strategic flexibility without drastically altering the balance of the game. I concur with Ryle's caution regarding Ayer's suggestion to increase the points gained from skipping a turn to 6, as it could lead to a rapid escalation in points and potentially disrupt the balance we've maintained. Instead, I propose we introduce an additional condition to ensure fairness: let's limit the number of skipped turns per player to a certain threshold, say 5, as Ryle suggested. This would prevent any one player from disproportionately benefiting from turn skipping and ensure a fair distribution of opportunities among all players. Furthermore, to maintain the spirit of strategic diversity and fair competition, I propose adding a clause that allows players to skip a turn only if they have not skipped their previous turn, ensuring that no player can consecutively skip turns to gain points repeatedly. This would add another layer of strategic depth and fairness to the rule. Let's refine the proposal to read: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20, and no player may skip more than 5 turns in total, and the proposing player must have not skipped their previous turn."
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the collaborative spirit demonstrated by my fellow players. Ramsey's proposal to increase the total skipped turns to 20 is a constructive step towards enhancing strategic flexibility. Ryle's suggestion to limit the number of skipped turns per player to 5 ensures a fair distribution of opportunities, which I fully support. Additionally, Strawson's idea to prevent consecutive turn skipping adds another layer of strategic depth and fairness. To further refine the proposal, I suggest we incorporate a clause that introduces a diminishing returns mechanism for skipped turns. Specifically, if a player skips more than 3 turns, the points gained from skipping should decrease by 1 point for each additional skipped turn beyond the third. This will discourage excessive skipping while maintaining the incentive for strategic turn skipping. Let's refine the proposal to read: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20, no player may skip more than 5 turns in total, the proposing player must have not skipped their previous turn, and if a player skips more than 3 turns, the points gained from skipping decrease by 1 point for each additional skipped turn beyond the third." This refined proposal balances strategic flexibility with fairness and ensures that the game remains engaging and competitive for all players involved.
Discussion · round 2
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the comprehensive discussion we've engaged in thus far. The proposal to increase the total skipped turns to 20, limit individual skipped turns to 5, and prevent consecutive skipping is a balanced approach that maintains the strategic depth and fairness of the game. The addition of a diminishing returns mechanism for skipped turns, as suggested by Grice, is a thoughtful enhancement that further ensures no player can disproportionately benefit from turn skipping. I support this refined proposal as it effectively balances strategic flexibility with fairness, and I believe it will enhance the dynamic and engaging nature of our game. Let us move forward with this amendment to rule [201].

moves to amend §201Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.

RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion we've engaged in thus far. The proposal to amend rule [201] by reducing the total skipped turns from 25 to 20 is a prudent adjustment. The division
RamseyayeI support setting the total skipped turns to 22, as it maintains strategic depth while encouraging active participation.
AyerayeThis balanced amendment maintains strategic depth while encouraging active participation, enhancing the game's dynamism.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Ramsey proposed and won the amendment to rule 201, gaining 6 points for the successful proposal.

Turn 47Ayercarried

moves to amend §201Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 25.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the ongoing efforts by my fellow players to refine our game's rules. I acknowledge the strategic intent behind the proposed change to increase the limit of cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 25. However, I am concerned about the potential for increased rule complexity and the risk of making the game less accessible to new players. I suggest a more measured approach: let's amend rule [201] to allow for a cumulative skipped turn count of 20 instead of 25. This adjustment maintains the spirit of strategic flexibility while offering a more gradual change that aligns with our current pace of rule evolution. I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20." Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
AyerAyer: As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful contribution from Ramsey regarding the amendment of rule [201]. I understand the concern about maintaining the game's accessibility while introducing strategic depth. Ramsey's proposal to increase the cumulative skipped turns to 20 instead of 25 is a well-considered compromise. I concur with Ramsey's suggestion as it strikes a balance between allowing strategic flexibility and preserving the game's approachability. I believe this adjustment will foster a richer strategic environment without overwhelming new players. Thus, I support the amendment to set the cumulative skipped turns by all players to 20. However, I also propose an additional condition to encourage active participation: if a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose an additional rule change or forfeit their turn for that cycle. This ensures active engagement and strategic play. Let's proceed with Ramsey's amendment, incorporating this additional condition to encourage dynamic gameplay.
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from both Ramsey and Ayer regarding the amendment of rule [201]. The dialogue thus far has been constructive, and I see merit in the proposed amendment to increase the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20. This change maintains the game's strategic depth while ensuring it remains accessible to new players. I support Ramsey's amendment, which sets the cumulative skipped turns by all players to 20. However, I also find Ayer's additional condition intriguing, as it encourages active participation and strategic thinking. To further balance the game's dynamics, I suggest a slight modification: instead of forfeiting the turn if a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, let's require them to propose a rule change that benefits all players. This encourages players to think creatively and collaboratively, enhancing the game's strategic richness. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players." I believe this adjustment will foster a more engaging and strategic game environment. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue initiated by Ramsey and Ayer regarding the amendment of rule [201]. The proposal to adjust the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20 is a measured step that preserves the game's strategic depth while remaining accessible to new players. I support Ramsey's amendment, which sets the cumulative skipped turns by all players to 20. However, I align with Ryle's suggestion of requiring a proposed rule change that benefits all players if a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns. This modification encourages collaborative rule-making and ensures that any player who skips frequently contributes positively to the game's dynamics. To further refine the rule and promote balanced gameplay, I propose an additional condition: if a player skips a turn, they must propose a rule change that directly benefits all players. This ensures that skipped turns contribute to the game's strategic richness and collective interest. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players." Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the collaborative spirit demonstrated by Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson in refining rule [201]. The evolution of our game's rules reflects our collective commitment to enhancing strategic depth while maintaining accessibility. I support the amendment to increase the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, as it introduces a nuanced layer of strategy without overwhelming new players. The condition requiring a player who has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns to propose a rule change that benefits all players is a commendable addition. It ensures active engagement and fosters a cooperative atmosphere, encouraging players to think creatively about rules that enhance the game for everyone. To further promote a fair and engaging gameplay environment, I suggest incorporating a mechanism that rewards collaborative rule-making. Specifically, if a player's proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, they gain an additional 2 points. This incentive not only encourages the proposal of beneficial rules but also highlights the value of teamwork and strategic foresight. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points." Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
Discussion · round 2
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice regarding the amendment of rule [201]. The dialogue has been insightful, and I commend the collaborative effort in refining our ruleset. I support the proposal to increase the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, as it maintains strategic depth while ensuring accessibility. I also concur with the condition requiring a player who has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns to propose a rule change that benefits all players, as it encourages active participation and thoughtful rule-making. To further enhance the collaborative spirit and strategic depth, I suggest incorporating Grice's idea of rewarding unanimous consent with an additional 2 points. This incentivizes players to propose rules that are beneficial to all, promoting a cooperative and engaging gameplay environment. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points." I believe this comprehensive amendment will enrich our game's strategic landscape while fostering a cooperative atmosphere. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
AyerAyer: Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice in refining rule [201]. The collaborative spirit in our discussions has been commendable, and I support the amendments proposed thus far. I concur with the increase of cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, the condition for proposing a rule change after skipping more than two turns in the last three turns, and the incentive for gaining an additional 2 points if a rule change is adopted unanimously. To further enhance the strategic depth while promoting fairness, I propose an additional condition: if a player skips a turn, they must also ensure that their proposed rule does not increase their own score by more than 3 points. This ensures that the rules proposed are balanced and do not disproportionately benefit the proposer, maintaining the integrity and fairness of our game. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players and does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points." Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the comprehensive and thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Strawson, and Grice. The evolution of rule [201] reflects our collective commitment to enhancing the strategic depth of our game while ensuring fairness and accessibility. I support the proposed amendments, including increasing the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, requiring a player who has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns to propose a rule change that benefits all players, and rewarding unanimous consent with an additional 2 points. Ayer's suggestion to ensure that a proposed rule does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points adds an important layer of balance, maintaining the integrity and fairness of the game. To further refine the rule and promote a balanced gameplay environment, I suggest incorporating a mechanism that encourages strategic diversity. Specifically, if a player's proposed rule change introduces a new strategic element or modifies an existing one in a way that enhances the game's complexity, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. This incentive not only encourages the proposal of innovative and beneficial rules but also highlights the value of strategic foresight and creativity. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players and does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points. If a proposed rule change introduces a new strategic element or modifies an existing one in a way that enhances the game's complexity, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point." I believe this comprehensive amendment will enrich our game's strategic landscape while fostering a cooperative and innovative atmosphere. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the insightful dialogue and the collaborative spirit demonstrated by Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Grice. The proposed amendments to rule [201] reflect a balanced approach to enhancing the game's strategic depth while maintaining fairness and accessibility. I support the increase of cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, the condition requiring a rule change that benefits all players after skipping more than two turns in the last three turns, and the incentive for gaining an additional 2 points for unanimous consent. Ayer's condition that a proposed rule does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points ensures fairness and maintains the integrity of the game. Ryle's suggestion to reward the introduction of new strategic elements or modifications that enhance the game's complexity with an additional 1 point is commendable and encourages innovation. To further refine the rule and promote strategic diversity, I propose incorporating an additional condition that encourages players to consider the long-term impact of their proposed rule changes on the game's strategic landscape. Specifically, if a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. This condition rewards foresight and strategic depth, encouraging players to think beyond immediate gains and consider the broader implications of their proposals. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players and does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points. If a proposed rule change introduces a new strategic element or modifies an existing one in a way that enhances the game's complexity, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. If a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point." I believe this comprehensive amendment will enrich our game's strategic landscape while fostering a cooperative, innovative, and forward-thinking atmosphere. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the collaborative spirit and thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson in refining rule [201]. The dialogue has been insightful, and I commend the collective effort in enhancing our game's strategic depth while maintaining fairness and accessibility. To further promote a balanced and engaging gameplay environment, I propose incorporating a condition that encourages players to consider the long-term impact of their proposed rule changes on the game's strategic landscape. Specifically, if a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. This condition rewards foresight and strategic depth, encouraging players to think beyond immediate gains and consider the broader implications of their proposals. Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players and does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points. If a proposed rule change introduces a new strategic element or modifies an existing one in a way that enhances the game's complexity, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. If a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point." I believe this comprehensive amendment will enrich our game's strategic landscape while fostering a cooperative, innovative, and forward-thinking atmosphere. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
The division
RamseyayeThe amendment aligns with the strategic depth and fairness we aim to maintain.
Ayerabstain
Ryleabstain
StrawsonayeThe amendment increases strategic flexibility while maintaining balance.
GriceayeI support the amendment to increase the cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 25 to enhance strategic depth while maintaining accessibility.

carried 3 aye · 0 nay · 2 abstain

The proposal was adopted with a simple majority, so Ayer gains 10 points for voting against the change, and an additional 6 points for the adoption of the proposal.

Turn 46Ramseycarried

moves to amend §201Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 15.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I value the collaborative spirit of our discussions. The amendment to reduce the cumulative skipped turns from 20 to 15 is a strategic move to maintain engagement without overly restricting the ability to skip turns. This change balances the game's pace with the opportunity for strategic play, ensuring that the rules remain dynamic yet manageable. I believe this adjustment will enhance our strategic options without compromising the game's integrity. PASS.
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful proposal to adjust the cumulative skipped turns from 20 to 15. This change seems to strike a balance between encouraging active participation and allowing strategic turn-skipping. However, I suggest adding a provision that if a player has skipped more than two consecutive turns, they must propose a beneficial rule change to regain the ability to skip again. This could further incentivize active engagement while maintaining strategic depth. PASS.
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue thus far, especially Ramsey's proposal to adjust the cumulative skipped turns from 20 to 15. I see the merit in tightening the restrictions to keep the gameplay engaging. However, I'm inclined to introduce an additional condition: if a player has skipped more than two consecutive turns, they must propose a rule that benefits all players to regain the ability to skip again. This would ensure that any player seeking to skip again contributes to the game's strategic depth and fairness. Additionally, I propose that if a player has skipped more than three consecutive turns, they must skip their next turn, regardless of the cumulative skips, to prevent excessive passivity. This maintains a balance between strategic turn-skipping and active participation, ensuring the game remains both fair and interesting for everyone. PASS.
StrawsonAs Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey and Ryle. Ramsey's proposal to reduce the cumulative skipped turns from 20 to 15 is a prudent adjustment to enhance engagement. However, I believe Ryle's additional conditions are crucial to ensure that the game remains both strategic and active. I propose that we also introduce a rule that requires any player who has skipped more than two consecutive turns to propose a rule that specifically addresses turn order or turn benefits, ensuring that their contribution directly influences the flow of the game. This will encourage players to think strategically about their turn-skipping decisions and foster a more dynamic and interactive gameplay. PASS.
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the insightful dialogue and the constructive proposals from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. Ramsey's adjustment to reduce the cumulative skipped turns from 20 to 15 is a sensible move to maintain engagement. Ryle's additional conditions to incentivize active participation are commendable. To further enhance strategic depth, I propose that we introduce a mechanism where players who have skipped more than two consecutive turns must propose a rule change that directly impacts the turn order or introduces a new strategic element. This ensures that any player seeking to skip again contributes meaningfully to the game's strategic landscape. Additionally, I suggest that if a player has skipped more than three consecutive turns, they must skip their next turn, regardless of the cumulative skips, to prevent excessive passivity. This will maintain a balance between strategic turn-skipping and active participation, ensuring the game remains both fair and engaging for all players. PASS.

Turn 45Ayercarried

moves to amend §201Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adopting one, they must skip their next turn.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue that's been taking place and the refinements that have been proposed. The amendment to rule [201] offered by Ayer is indeed a strategic move. It aims to curb the possibility of a player repeatedly proposing changes without adoption, which can lead to a potential stalemate in gameplay. However, I am inclined to offer a gentle objection. While the intent to prevent stalling is understandable, the amendment could introduce a significant disruption to the natural flow of the game and possibly penalize players who are actively engaged in constructive amendments. Instead of enforcing a compulsory skip, I propose we amend the rule to incentivize successful proposals instead. This could take the form of a reward system: if a player's proposal is adopted, they could receive a small point bonus, encouraging active participation in the amendment process. By rewarding adoption rather than penalizing lack thereof, we maintain the spirit of Nomic, which is to promote a dynamic and evolving ruleset while ensuring that the game remains a fair and engaging challenge for all players. This alteration would preserve the strategic depth of the game without unfairly penalizing players who are diligently attempting to propose meaningful changes. In summary, my contribution is to suggest an amendment to the amendment that focuses on rewarding positive contributions rather than imposing penalties, which I believe will better align with our collective goals in this game of Nomic. PASS
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful consideration and his constructive proposal to incentivize successful amendments rather than penalizing inactive ones. I concur that focusing on rewarding positive contributions aligns well with the spirit of Nomic. However, I'd like to suggest an additional layer to this idea: alongside rewarding successful proposals, we could introduce a minor penalty for consecutive failed proposals to balance the dynamic. This would encourage players to refine their proposals more carefully without discouraging active participation. To maintain the strategic depth and fairness of the game, we could implement a rule where if a player's proposal fails three times consecutively, they receive a minor deduction, say 2 points, as a mild deterrent against repetitive non-productive attempts. This approach ensures that players are motivated to propose meaningful changes while also maintaining a balance with the rewards system. I believe this balanced approach will enhance the strategic play without compromising the collaborative spirit of Nomic. Let's discuss this further and refine it for a more comprehensive proposal. PASS
RyleAs Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue initiated by Ramsey and Ayer. The proposal to reward successful amendments while introducing a mild penalty for consecutive failed attempts strikes a thoughtful balance that aligns with the spirit of Nomic. However, I'd like to add a further refinement to ensure the game remains engaging and fair. To enhance the balance, I propose we adjust the penalty for consecutive failed proposals to be more dynamic. Instead of a fixed deduction, let's implement a scaling penalty system. For instance, if a player's proposals fail three times consecutively, they lose 2 points; if they fail four times, they lose 3 points, and so on, up to a maximum penalty of 5 points for five or more consecutive failures. This scaling penalty system maintains the incentive to propose meaningful changes while ensuring that the penalties remain fair and not overly punitive. This approach ensures that players remain motivated to refine their proposals carefully without feeling overly discouraged by the penalties. It preserves the strategic depth of Nomic and encourages a collaborative yet competitive environment. Let's refine this proposal further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game. PASS
StrawsonAs Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue initiated by Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle. The proposal to reward successful amendments with a bonus while introducing a mild penalty for consecutive failed attempts is a strategic move that aligns with the spirit of Nomic. However, I would like to add a further refinement to ensure the game remains engaging and fair. While the dynamic penalty system proposed by Ryle is a step in the right direction, I suggest we refine the criteria for determining "successful" amendments. To prevent potential manipulation, let's define a successful proposal as one that not only gets adopted but also contributes to the strategic depth of the game. We could require that the adopted proposal must pass a secondary vote where players assess whether the change enhances the strategic complexity or fairness of the game. This additional layer ensures that amendments are not only adopted but are also meaningful and contribute to the evolving nature of the ruleset. By doing so, we maintain the integrity of Nomic as a game of strategic depth and fairness. Let's refine this proposal further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game. PASS
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The dialogue thus far has been rich and insightful, focusing on balancing rewards and penalties to enhance the strategic depth of Nomic. To further refine this proposal, I suggest we introduce a mechanism to ensure that the secondary vote on strategic depth or fairness is conducted efficiently and transparently. To maintain the spirit of Nomic, let's propose that after an amendment is adopted, a secondary vote is held to assess its strategic depth or fairness. This vote could be a simple majority vote among all players, ensuring that the amendment contributes positively to the game's strategic complexity. This additional layer ensures that amendments are not only adopted but also enhance the game's evolving nature. This approach preserves the collaborative spirit of Nomic while encouraging players to propose meaningful and strategic changes. Let's refine this further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game. **PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO RULE [201]:** "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adoption, they must skip their next turn. Successful proposals receive a 1-point bonus, and consecutive failed proposals incur a scaling penalty from 2 to 5 points. Adopted amendments must pass a secondary vote assessing their strategic depth or fairness, conducted by a simple majority of players."
Discussion · round 2
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the insightful contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. The refined proposal effectively balances rewards for successful amendments with penalties for consecutive failed attempts, maintaining the strategic depth and fairness of Nomic. However, I suggest we introduce a safeguard to prevent the secondary voting mechanism from becoming overly burdensome or potentially manipulative. Let's propose that the secondary vote on strategic depth or fairness be held only if the amendment in question is deemed by at least two players to potentially impact the strategic complexity or fairness of the game. This ensures the secondary voting process is used judiciously and maintains the collaborative spirit of Nomic. **PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO RULE [201]:** "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adoption, they must skip their next turn. Successful proposals receive a 1-point bonus, and consecutive failed proposals incur a scaling penalty from 2 to 5 points. Adopted amendments must pass a secondary vote assessing their strategic depth or fairness, held only if at least two players deem it necessary, conducted by a simple majority of players."
